data.title: Meta Platforms: A Growing Risk Investors Can't Keep Ignoring
data.description:
Following the recent release of Meta Platforms' (NASDAQ:META) Q1 2025 earnings report, investors bid up its stock price as the market gained confidence that the company's AI investments are paying off through better recommendation systems and users' time spent. While this is certainly encouraging, Meta also faces a growing risk amid the rising popularity of generative AI apps, which threaten to expand into the social media space and eat into the company's digital advertising market share over time.

In the previous article, we had discussed how Meta Platforms' conversational AI assistant "Meta AI" is falling behind rivals like ChatGPT in terms of usage volumes, undermining the social media giant's challenge to these new-age AI apps. We also covered the looming risk of Meta potentially having to divest Instagram and WhatsApp due to antitrust litigation, which would be a big blow to the company given how important these apps are to win in the generative AI era.

Since then, a new risk has arisen, with OpenAI contemplating launching its own social media app by leveraging the existing popularity of its ChatGPT assistant and image-generation tool that has gone viral over the past few months.

Meta stock maintains a "hold" rating.

New AI apps Are a Force to Be Reckoned With

When people talk about the rapid growth of OpenAI, they immediately think about the threat it poses to Google Search, as the functionalities of ChatGPT have expanded to Search verticals like "Maps" and "News". Although with OpenAI increasingly pushing out new creative tools like image/ video generators, the rising AI app also threatens to take away time spent on Meta's social media apps like Instagram, Facebook and WhatsApp.

Last month, OpenAI shared that ChatGPT had reached 800 million weekly users. While Meta Platforms has not yet shared how many weekly users its "Meta AI" assistant has garnered, CEO Mark Zuckerberg did mention on the recent earnings call that "there are now almost a billion monthly actives using Meta AI". That's more than Gemini's 350 million monthly users revealed in April, although Meta has still not been able to dethrone ChatGPT as the top AI app despite boasting 3.4 billion daily users across its social media apps.

The reason why the use of ChatGPT has been proliferating at an accelerated pace over the past few months is due to the new image-generation tool it launched at the end of March 2025.

The company debuted the "high-quality" image-generation tool as a way to produce everything from diagrams, infographics and logos to business cards and stock photos. The feature can also use an image as a starting point for art, such as a custom painting of a pet or editing a professional headshot.

…

Images of anime-style renderings of users' uploaded photos have been going viral on X and other social media apps

- CNBC

In just over a week after the feature launched, OpenAI's COO Brad Lightcap shared on April 3rd that:

Over 130 million users have generated more than 700 million images since the upgraded image generator launched in ChatGPT on March 25.

- TechCrunch

This is the kind of viral popularity that Meta wishes it could garner for its own generative AI features that rolled out over the past two years.

In the autumn of 2023, Meta had introduced experimental AI features called "AI Personas", whereby users could interact with digital avatars of celebrities like Snoop Dogg and Kendall Jenner, as well as non-celebrity AI personalities. However, both these experiences have been discontinued following user backlash.

Meta removes AI character accounts after users criticize them as 'creepy and unnecessary'

In July 2024, Meta scrapped its celebrity AI characters and launched AI Studio, a way for people to create their own AI characters that can also be accessed by other users through the messaging functions on Meta's social media platforms.

- NBC News

The social media giant had also launched an "Imagine me" feature for creative selfies in July 2024, following the popularity of the "Lensa AI" app.

However, almost a year after launching these generative AI experiences, executives haven't offered any statistical insights into usage volumes the way OpenAI frequently reveals for new features.

The lack of traction for Meta's own generative AI experiences means it is vulnerable to being competitively challenged by new social media apps that are likely to arise in this new era of generative AI.

In fact, building upon the popularity of ChatGPT and its image-generation tool, there have been reports that OpenAI is considering entering the social media space:

OpenAI, the generative artificial intelligence startup backed by Microsoft (MSFT) and others, is working on building a social network, The Verge reported.

The project is still in the early stages, but an internal prototype includes a social feed that is focused on ChatGPT's image generation, the news outlet added, citing sources familiar with the matter.

- Seeking Alpha News

This presents a massive risk to Meta, which is already struggling to ignite user excitement around its own AI features. If OpenAI's endeavor in the social media space proves successful based off of the already-popular image-generation tool, it could further subvert the time spent on Meta's suite of apps, just like how TikTok has been a big headache over the past several years.

Moreover, OpenAI also made advances last month to facilitate more shopping activities through ChatGPT, by surfacing product recommendations in response to commercial queries from users.

Just like OpenAI, rising AI Search engine "Perplexity" also recently expanded into facilitating commerce activities, and both companies have partnerships with Shopify (SHOP) to buoy these endeavors.

So these new-age AI apps, particularly OpenAI, are incrementally encroaching onto Meta Platforms' territory, as they strive to carve out a slice of the digital advertising market for themselves.

Furthermore, a survey of 12,000 consumers by consultancy firm "Capgemini" revealed that:

More than half (58%) have replaced traditional search engines with Gen AI tools as their go-to for product/service recommendations. 68% of consumers want Gen AI tools to aggregate search results from online search engines, social media platforms, and retailers' websites to provide a one-stop shop for highlighted purchase options.

- Capgemini

The second statistic is particularly concerning, as over two-thirds of surveyed consumers don't want to bother with visiting different social media apps and retail webstores to find the product that they want.

Instead, they are asking for chatbots (and eventually AI agents) to scour through social media pages on apps like Instagram, Facebook, TikTok and Pinterest, as well as other sources, and aggregate product recommendations that fit their queries.

So instead of just humans browsing on social media platforms, over time it could increasingly be AI agents skimming through these apps to serve users' shopping intentions.

Earlier this year, OpenAI already introduced the "Operator" AI agent that works at the browser system level to conduct both research and commercial activities on behalf of users. And Perplexity is anticipated to launch its agentic "Comet" browser this month.

AI Agents facilitating commerce activities for users could indeed distort the digital advertising landscape for companies like Meta Platforms, which have traditionally designed product recommendations and advertisements for humans.

Additionally, while the "Capgemini" citation earlier highlighted that 58% of consumers overall now turn to "Gen AI" tools for shopping queries, this number jumps to 65% for Gen-Z consumers, and 67% for Millennials.

So the threat of OpenAI (and potentially other generative AI start-ups) stepping into Meta Platforms' territory should not be taken lightly.

While social media apps are primarily destinations for social interactions and entertainment, Meta Platforms has been successfully cultivating its apps into prominent shopping destinations as well. However, the rise of new, generative AI-powered chatbots and social networks could throw a spanner into the works of Meta on this front.

Meta Platforms Is Fighting Back

Despite the rise of new generative AI apps, it's not as if commerce activities through social media platforms are going to completely vanish. After all, these platforms have given rise to influencers that are increasingly inducing their followers to engage in shopping activities.

Social media influencers are also becoming popular, with around 7 in 10 Gen Zs learning about new products through them in 2024, up significantly from 45% in 2023.

Platforms such as Instagram and TikTok are also reshaping retail, with over half of consumers discovering new products via social media, up from 32% in November 2022.

- Capgemini

Furthermore, Meta has been leveraging the power of AI to continuously enhance the predictive accuracy of its recommendation systems to display the most relevant content to users on their feeds.

In the last six months, improvements to our recommendation systems have led to a 7% increase in time spent on Facebook, a 6% increase on Instagram, and 35% on Threads. Threads now also has more than 350 million monthly actives and continues to be on track to become our next major social app.

- CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Meta Platforms Q1 2025 earnings call

These remarks ignited investors to push the stock price of META higher post-earnings, as it offered evidence that the social media giant's massive investments into AI are paying off.

Additionally, even if users don't intentionally open Meta's apps for shopping purposes, people spending more time on entertaining content gives the company incremental opportunities to show ads, and also keeps the window open for commerce activities through social media influencers.

Meta undoubtedly has a lot more experience at perfecting recommendation systems than OpenAI and other new-age challengers.

Another area where Meta has a lot more experience is connecting businesses with the right consumers for targeted advertising. The company has been using generative AI to make the advertising process a lot smoother for businesses.

Our goal is to make it so that any business can basically tell us what objective they're trying to achieve like selling something or getting a new customer and how much they're willing to pay for each result, and then we just do the rest.

Businesses used to have to generate their own ad creative and define what audiences they wanted to reach, but AI has already made us better at targeting and finding the audiences that will be interested in their products than many businesses are themselves, and that keeps improving. And now AI is generating better creative options for many businesses as well.

…

In just the last quarter, we are testing a new ads recommendation model for reels, which has already increased conversion rates by 5%, and we're seeing 30% more advertisers are using AI creative tools in the last quarter as well.

- CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Meta Platforms Q1 2025 earnings call

So while OpenAI and Perplexity are taking steps to enhance the commercial potency of their platforms to attract advertising dollars in the future, Meta possesses a treasure of data accumulated over many years that it is now leveraging in the generative AI era, enabling advertisers to target consumers more accurately.

The Investment Case for Meta Platforms

Meta Platforms delivered total revenue growth of 17.5% in Q1 2025. More importantly, Family of Apps revenue grew by over 16%, which actually marks a slowdown from the same quarter last when it grew by almost 26%.

Nonetheless, investors were impressed by Meta's strengthening pricing power for its ad solutions:

The average price per ad increased 10%... Pricing growth benefited from increased advertiser demand, in part driven by improved ad performance. - CFO Susan Li, Meta Platforms Q1 2025 earnings call

Indeed, Meta's massive investments into AI infrastructure is presently paying off in the form of more precise recommendation systems, conducive to more relevant content and ads being shown to users.

Although AI gains are expected to stay limited to recommendation system advancements over the near future, as executives have signaled that they don't expect their generative AI-powered features (such as the "Meta AI" assistant and "AI Studio" which allows users to build their own AI personas) to become monetizable in 2025.

This year the improvements of the business are going to be taking the AI methods and applying them to advertising and recommendations and feeds and things like that. So the actual business opportunity for Meta AI and AI studio and business agents and people interacting with these AIs remains outside of '25 for the most part.

- CEO Mark Zuckerberg, Q4 2024 earnings call

Before Meta can even get to monetize its generative AI features, it will first need to accelerate user engagement, as these experiences across the suite of apps are still not garnering the traction that OpenAI is witnessing.

In terms of EPS growth, Meta is expected to grow at 17.42% CAGR over the next 3–5 years.

While Meta's projected EPS growth rate is greater than that of chief advertising rival Google, it is lower than that of Amazon, which also competes against Meta for advertising dollars.

Based on these projected EPS growth rates, below are the Forward Price-Earnings-Growth (Forward PEG) ratios for the Magnificent 7 stocks.

A Forward PEG multiple of 1x would implicate that a stock is trading at around fair value.

So with the king of AI, Nvidia, trading well below fair value, the other Mag 7 names naturally appear expensive to own.

Although there is a growing narrative that the AI trade will shift from hardware to software stocks this year. But this narrative isn't necessarily backed up by EPS growth projections, as Nvidia's expected 35%+ EPS growth (3–5Y CAGR) still far outpaces the forecasts for the other AI players.

Microsoft, Amazon and Google each possess cloud service platforms which also serve as the "picks and shovels" of this AI revolution. This has enabled MSFT in particular to command a premium valuation relative to the other Mag 7 stocks, with its financial performance buoyed by OpenAI's success as well.

On the other hand, Meta, Google and Amazon are simultaneously at risk from new-age AI apps like OpenAI and Perplexity distorting the way consumers embark on their shopping journeys.

And unlike its chief advertising rivals, Meta Platforms does not have a cloud services business of its own to benefit from during the transformation phase across companies and industries.

Instead, Meta is solely reliant on its generative AI-powered social media features driving user engagement and future ad dollars. So far, this narrative has not yet taken off.

Meanwhile, OpenAI reportedly generated revenue of $3.7 billion in 2024, and expects to bring in a staggering $12.7 billion in 2025. While this still dwarfs in comparison to Meta Platforms, which produced over $164 billion in revenue last year, investors cannot ignore the fast pace at which the new-age AI companies are growing.

Over the past several quarters throughout 2024, the advertising revenue growth rates for Meta, Google and Amazon have been slowing down, following robust growth rates in 2023. Looking forward, any re-acceleration in ad revenue growth rates would depend on their AI features witnessing usage growth among consumers.

High-quality tech stocks with promising growth stories and strong moats tend to trade at Forward PEG multiples of 2x or above.

Although at present, out of the Mag 7 it is Microsoft and Apple that are trading above 2x Forward PEG, while Tesla is in a league of its own at almost 8x.

In order for Meta to be able to command a higher valuation multiple than the 1.44x it is currently trading at, it needs to deliver robust user engagement growth for its latest generative AI experiences, amid the growing competitive threats from new-age AI apps like OpenAI. Any uptrend in usage of features like "AI studio" and "Meta AI", particularly for commercial purposes, would give investors the confidence they need that advertising revenue will continue to grow into the future.

For now, Meta stock maintains a 'Hold' rating.

Commentary as of 03/31/25

  • The fund posted returns of -1.02% (Institutional shares) and -1.07% (Investor A shares, without sales charge) for the first quarter of 2025.
  • The fund’s outperformance of its benchmark was led by select sentiment and fundamental quality insights, which helped drive successful positioning across Europe and Japan. However, U.S. security selection struggled amid changing leadership dynamics.
  • From a sector- and country-positioning perspective, the fund remained largely neutral. It had slight overweight allocations to the information technology and financials sectors, and maintained slight underweight exposures to the consumer discretionary and utilities sectors. The fund had a slight overweight position in the United Kingdom and maintained a slight underweight exposure to the United States.
Contributors Detractors
Sentiment-related insights capturing “smart-money” positioning drove gains. These insights overcame sharp derisking in early March as investors rotated out of previous momentum winners. Fundamental quality measures focused on sustainability of earnings were also top contributors. Other stability-related fundamental insights with a preference for lower volatility stocks and dividend consistency were additive. Collectively, stock selection was strong across Europe through a preference for domestic exposure via the financials sector and the aerospace and defense industry within the industrials sector over those reliant on global trade. These gains were supported by positioning in Japanese banks, driven by accelerating inflation and rising rates expectations. In contrast to positioning abroad, security selection in the United States came under pressure, notably in the IT and industrials sectors. Traditional fundamental valuation insights tracking sales, valuations, and other financial statement data drove losses. Notably, positioning in the IT sector began to evolve toward artificial intelligence adopters over enablers, which helped stem losses in March as large-cap technology winners capitulated. Elsewhere, trend following insights, both macro and analyst and management sentiment, struggled against changing leadership dynamics. Similarly, consumer intent-related sentiment measures evaluating online search trends, social media data, and product-ranking trends all proved wrong-footed.

Further insight

After touching all-time highs in February, global equities declined sharply to end in negative territory. U.S. market stress drove the sell-off as uncertainty about the new administration’s policy agenda simultaneously stoked recession concerns and market volatility. Combined with cooling sentiment toward the AI opportunity, after Chinese companies released rival, low-cost AI models, market leadership rotated as elevated U.S. valuations came under pressure. Unsurprisingly, despite concerns about inflation resulting from tariffs, economic growth worries drove a sharp drop in bond yields. In contrast, international markets proved resilient as investors repositioned for the end of the U.S. exceptionalism theme. European markets were the main beneficiary, despite sluggish economic data and corporate revenues overly exposed to tariff risks.

data.title: ArcelorMittal: Not Enough Upside To Justify The Risks
data.description:
Thesis

ArcelorMittal (NYSE:MT) is a large integrated and globally diversified steel and mining company with facilities spanning multiple continents. While ArcelorMittal may appear undervalued on some metrics such as having low price to net asset value ratio, a high 8-year average earnings yield and potential upside from future steelmaking expansion, its 10-year stock price performance has been underwhelming and it is exposed to significant risks. It has high variability in earnings including years with negative earnings and so it is vulnerable to negative earnings during a recession, it has significant tariff risk whose impact has yet to be felt, and it mainly operates carbon intensive integrated blast furnaces for its steel production exposing it to carbon risk. There are better alternative companies to invest in, and therefore, I rate this company as a sell.

Company Overview

ArcelorMittal is a large integrated steel and mining company operating in 15 countries across Europe, the Americas and Asia. It produced around 58 million tons of steel in 2023 and 2024 (see figure below). This compares to the 1,885 million tons of steel produced globally of which 1,005 million tons was produced in China, which means that ArcelorMittal produced around 3.1 percent of the world’s steel and around 6.6 percent of non Chinese steel. ArcelorMittal produced 43.5 million tons of steel using basic oxygen furnaces and 14.4 million tons of steel using electric arc furnaces. Most of ArcelorMittal’s steel production occurred in Europe at 31.2 million tons and Brazil at 14.6 million tons, with only 7.5 million tons of steel production occurring in North America. However, ArcelorMittal is in the process of constructing a new electric arc furnace (EAF) facility in Alabama, and is targeting long run annual production capacity of 40 million tons at its Indian steel plants, giving it plenty of steel production upside. It also produced around 44 million tons of iron ore in 2023 and 2024 to feed its steelmaking facilities, making it a large iron ore producer. It produced around 1.8 percent of the 2.5 billion tons of global iron ore production.

ArcelorMittal’s mines make it somewhat self sufficient in sourcing metals and minerals for its steelmaking facilities (see figure below). It was around 50%-60% self-sufficient in iron ore and scrap & direct reduced iron (DRI), and while it did not source any coal from its mines, it was 90% self sufficient in coke production. Its main iron ore mines are Mount Gangra in Liberia and Mont Wright in Canada. ArcelorMittal’s capacity in its Mount Gangra mine was 5 million tons in 2024, but this could potentially expand to 30 million tons of capacity. Its Mont Wright mine is in the Labrador Trough and produces around 26 million tons of high-grade iron ore high annually. This iron ore is upgradable to DRI material, which is crucial for meeting the demand of EAF plants moving forward.

Given that steel production accounts for around 8 percent of global carbon emissions, many steel manufacturers, including ArcelorMittal, are in the process of reducing their carbon emissions mainly through increasing the share of steel produced using EAF (see figure below). However, EAF can only use steel scrap and DRI grade iron ore material as inputs, making it essential for companies such as ArcelorMittal to secure enough supply of this material. ArcelorMittal’s carbon reduction initiatives have therefore included securing more steel scrap and increasing the output of DR pellets from its Mont Wright mine.

Comparing ArcelorMittal to its main competitors using the following peer list from Seeking Alpha as a source, ArcelorMittal’s stock price performance has not been particularly good either on a relative or absolute basis. Its stock price, while volatile, has not gone anywhere in the past 10 years. By comparison Nucor Corp (NUE) and Steel Dynamic Inc (STLD) are up around 134 percent and 503 percent respectively. Nucor and Steel Dynamics differ from ArcelorMittal in only using EAFs for their steelmaking process which could have provided them with a business and cost advantage over their competitors, and may help explain why steel manufacturers are switching to EAFs. Posco Holdings Inc (PKX) like ArcelorMittal operates a mix of integrated blast furnaces and EAFs. Posco performed worse than ArcelorMittal and is down around 20 percent over the 10-year period.

Company Financials

ArcelorMittal’s fiscal year aligns with the calendar year and their most recent annual report is from 2024. ArcelorMittal recorded positive net income for 2024 as well as for 2023 and 2022. ArcelorMittal’s net income in 2022 was impressive, but net income, particularly compared to revenues, was less impressive in 2023 and 2024. Net income and revenues for 2024 were $1.4 billion and $62.4 billion respectively, giving a net margin of just 2.2 percent. While ArcelorMittal was successful in lowering cost of sales from 2022 to 2024, revenue declined even more, leading to margin compression. Given that ArcelorMittal’s current market capitalization is around $26 billion, the resulting price to earnings (P/E) ratio is around 19, and so it is therefore hard to argue that ArcelorMittal is undervalued by this metric.

Examining ArcelorMittal’s most recent operations sheet from 2025 Quarter 1, sales and earnings for 2025 Quarter 1, look similar to the sales and earnings for 2024 Quarter 1. Therefore the trailing 12 month (TTM) P/E ratio would be fairly similar to the 2024 P/E of 19. Summing the net income from 2024 Q2 to 2025 Q1 and dividing by a market capitalization of $26 billion, gives a TTM P/E ratio of 22.

ArcelorMittal’s balance sheet as of December 31, 2024 looks quite strong, with its cash and cash equivalents coming close to matching its long-term debt. Its primary assets are inventories, property, plant and equipment and biological assets, and investments in associates and joint ventures, while its primary liabilities are trade accounts payable and other and long term debt. Assets totaled $89.4 billion and liabilities totaled $38.1 billion, resulting in a net asset value (NAV) of $51.3 billion. Using a market capitalization for ArcelorMittal of around $26 billion gives ArcelorMittal a price to NAV ((P/NAV)) ratio of around 0.51.

Looking at ArcelorMittal's most recent balance sheet from 2025 Quarter 1, it still looks strong. Cash and cash equivalents are down slightly, but most other asset categories are up, resulting in a slightly higher total asset value. Liabilities are largely unchanged. Assets totaled $91.5 billion and liabilities totaled $38.2 billion, resulting in a net asset value (NAV) of $53.3 billion. Using a market capitalization for ArcelorMittal of around $26 billion gives ArcelorMittal a most recent price to NAV ((P/NAV)) ratio of around 0.49.

Company Valuations

To evaluate whether ArcelorMittal is a buy or a sell, it is useful to compare its valuation with that of its competitors. Using information on stock market capitalization and shareholder equity from Seeking Alpha, I estimate a P/NAV of 2.19 for Steel Dynamics, 1.24 for Nucor and 0.36 for Posco. This makes ArcelorMittal with a P/NAV of around 0.5 relatively undervalued compared to its peers.

Given that ArcelorMittal had a fairly high P/E ratio in 2024 and consequently a fairly low earnings yield, a higher earnings yield among its competitors would confirm that ArcelorMittal is overvalued on that metric. Interestingly, ArcelorMittal has the highest average earnings yield among it peers (see table below) albeit with a lot more variability. ArcelorMittal had very high earnings yields in 2021 and 2022, but also had negative earnings yields for 2019 and 2020. By comparison, all of ArcelorMittal’s peers had positive earnings yields each year over the 2017 to 2024 time period. Furthermore, the earnings yields for Nucor and Steel Dynamics were higher than ArcelorMittal's earnings yields in 2023 and 2024.

Investment Risks

Based on the information presented above ArcelorMittal looks like a buy based on P/NAV, average earnings yields and higher potential steel production, and a sell based on 10-year price performance and high variability in earnings. Therefore, it is useful to investigate potential risks to ArcelorMittal and how those risks impact its peers. These risks include tariffs, the high carbon intensity of the steel industry and an economic downturn. For all three risks ArcelorMittal’s downside appears larger than those of Nucor and Steel Dynamics, and therefore ArcelorMittal seems like a worse buy than its competitors.

ArcelorMittal global footprint makes it vulnerable to a tariff war, particularly since steel tariffs have been imposed both in 2018 and 2025. It is likely that tariffs played a role in ArcelorMittal’s decision to sell most of their U.S. assets to Cleveland Cliffs in 2020. While this sale may make ArcelorMittal less vulnerable to tariffs in 2025, its global footprint still makes it more vulnerable than competitors Nucor and Steel Dynamics, who are predominately U.S. based are therefore would benefit from higher steel tariffs. On the other hand, tariffs of steel may be reduced if the reduction in tariffs between the U.S. and China announced on May 12, 2025 offer any indication. If tariffs were reduced, this might benefit ArcelorMittal at the expense of Nucor and Steel Dynamics. However, Nucor and Steel Dynamics already had a higher earnings yield than ArcelorMittal in 2024 before the 2025 steel tariffs were implemented.

The steel industry faces a challenge with reducing carbon emissions, and companies such as ArcelorMittal are addressing those challenges by increasing the share of steel produced using EAF and sourcing additional supplies of DRI and steel scrap. However, the EAF share of their production was still only 25% in 2024, compared to Nucor and Steel Dynamics, which exclusively use EAF to produce their steel. Therefore, while ArcelorMittal is making progress on its carbon emissions goals, it is still far behind its competitors Nucor and Steel Dynamics, and is therefore more exposed to this carbon emission risk. Potentially one of the reasons why ArcelorMittal has a lower P/NAV ratio than its EAF focused competitors, is that the asset value of its integrated blast furnaces may be overvalued given their carbon risk.

Given that many analysts are predicting an imminent economic downturn, it does not seem from ArcelorMittal’s P/E ratio that its stock price is reflecting this risk. ArcelorMittal’s competitors are also exposed to this risk, and therefore the stock prices of all the steel companies may fall substantially if a recession does occur. However, given that ArcelorMittal was the only company among its peers that had negative earnings yields in the last 8 years, it may again be more likely than its competitor to have a negative earnings yield if a downturn does occur, and consequently its share price may fall more.

Final Thoughts

While ArcelorMittal is arguably a sell when compared to investing in its competitors Nucor and Steel Dynamics, there are other stocks that I would rate even more favorably. One of these is Labrador Iron Ore Royalty Corp (LIORC) (OTCPK:LIFZF) which I covered here and here. First, it is a royalty company, and royalty companies have a long history of outperforming other mining and metals companies due to the advantages of the royalty model. Second, it has a royalty on a long life high grade iron ore mine in Canada, which can produce DRI grade material to feed the EAF plants. Given that there is only so much scrap steel to obtain, the demand for DRI material is expected to grow rapidly, greatly benefiting companies like LIORC.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Built To Last: 2 Of My Favorite Dividend Stocks For The Next Decade
data.description:
Introduction

Last month, I wrote an article titled "Buckle Up Buttercup [...]." While it wasn't aimed at predicting very specific market movements, it got the market sentiment right, as we have been through a ton of volatility in the first few months of this year.

This includes one of the worst trading weeks in decades, one of the most volatile volatility spikes in history, and one of the strongest short-term rebounds since at least the 1990s.

After the United States and China agreed on a trade war truce, the market has been on a mission to erase past losses, triggering one of the biggest rallies since the President announced a 90-day tariff pause more than 20 days ago.

Personally, I am (obviously) quite happy with these developments, as I have been an aggressive buyer in April, deploying a big chunk of my cash reserves to buy mostly cyclical (value) stocks, the one sector I believe will help us generate tremendous returns for many years to come.

While this is by no means a victory lap, my portfolio is now up slightly more than 9% on a year-to-date basis, after being down more than 5% during the worst days of April, making it one of the strongest rebounds in a long time.

The best thing about this news is that the main risks to the market were what Apollo Global Management called a "Voluntary Trade Reset Recession."

While I am not saying the President was right or wrong (I'll leave this up to his voters), most risks were self-inflicted, meaning the market was fully aware that a change of mind in the nation's capital could also reverse these risks.

In other words, unlike the pandemic or a widespread financial crisis, politicians were in a much better spot to ease the pain. It's like holding an ice cube. After a while, it really starts to hurt. However, we can ease the pain by simply letting go.

As silly as that may sound, the Polymarket recession indicator shows that this has just happened to the recession odds, which dropped from almost 70% back below 40%. At this point, we're unlikely to encounter a recession this year. All else being equal, of course.

In general, we're seeing a return to 'normal', including outperformance of stocks versus bonds, a steep decline in volatility, and outperformance of S&P 500 stocks compared to the rest of the world. The only thing that's still lagging is oil compared to gold, as the world's biggest energy commodity is still being pressured by OPEC's decision to boost output.

So far, so good.

The problem is that we're not out of the woods yet.

For starters, most forget that stocks were down roughly 7% before President Trump's 'Liberation Day' caused stocks to crater. This decline resulted from fundamental issues such as a lofty stock market valuation, weakening growth, and geopolitical concerns, sparked by fears of a decoupling of the U.S. from some of its major Western partners on key issues like defense.

This is what The Wall Street Journal wrote in light of the market's challenging risk/reward, using numbers from a day or two before the Chinese trade war truce news (this is key to keep in mind here):

Crucially, forward profit expectations for the S&P 500 and technology stocks in particular were already being downgraded before the trade war started. For reference, recessions typically involve a fall in earnings of 20% or more.

Assuming a very benign scenario in which earnings-per-share growth fell simply to the five-year average of 7.9% and the forward P/E ratio rose back to the maximum around which it has hovered in recent years, which is 22, the S&P 500 would still have only about 6% upside. That isn’t much when cash yields 4%. - WSJ (emphasis added)

In other words, what the economy needs now is a path to higher growth with inflation coming down further to allow the Fed to ease financial conditions. Such a scenario could continue to fuel this rally, I think.

Even with stocks at pre-Liberation Day levels, recession risks haven’t gone away, and Northern Trust puts the odds as high as 60% on a mild downturn. A decent starting position for household and business activity should limit any ensuing downturn. A rapid easing of financial conditions is also helpful, and SMBC Nikko Securities’ Joe Lavorgna, an official in the first Trump administration, argues that the Fed should be able to lower interest rates if inflation eases on the back of substantially lower oil prices:

- Bloomberg (emphasis added)

While recent developments are very positive for the market and the economy, we are not out of the woods yet, which is why I have started building a cash position again, with a bigger focus on stocks that come with a mix of deep value, safety, consistent dividend growth, and business models that can help us to beat the market for many years to come.

In this article, I'll give you two of my favorites.

Rexford Industrial Realty (REXR) - One Of The Biggest Winners

I have covered hundreds of individual stocks in recent years. However, every once in a while, I increase my focus on a smaller number of stocks. This sometimes leads to criticism from some readers who accuse me of running out of ideas.

I'm doing this because some stocks simply fit my strategy for certain economic environments. It's the same reason Mike Tomlin has been the head coach of the Pittsburgh Steelers since 2007, making him the third coach hired by the organization since 1969. If it works, don't change it.

In other words, I prefer to focus on the best of the best, putting quality over variety, although I am obviously committed to presenting a wide variety of stocks to a diverse range of investor types.

Last year, I started buying Rexford Industrial Realty because it fit my strategy of buying undervalued real estate equities, as I was very bullish on energy and hard assets like real estate REITs.

Then, it became the ground zero of the trade war because 100% of its 424 industrial properties are located in Southern California's infill markets. This market is home to the San Pedro Port Complex (Los Angeles + Long Beach), the biggest in the United States, responsible for roughly a third of all container imports.

So, as you can imagine, a de facto trade embargo with China would be a huge deal. It also doesn't help that one in nine jobs is tied to this port, which means a long-term trade war would have done tremendous damage to domestic consumption.

That's exactly the market Rexford targets, as it owns smaller industrial properties under 50,000 square feet that cater to local supply chains in warehousing, industrial production, aerospace, healthcare, and others.

As a result, REXR shares lost roughly a quarter of their value this year.

That's why I bought a lot more in April. While I did not know the trade war would get a big break this soon, I simply liked the risk/reward. And I still do.

That's why REXR is included in this article.

For example, now that the trade war has shifted into a much lower gear, trade flows are expected to rapidly accelerate, as experts like Ryan Petersen note.

On top of that, while Southern California has political issues, it is still a market that comes with the biggest industrial base in the United States, one of the biggest consumer markets on the planet, and highly favorable supply/demand dynamics, as the entire region is wedged between the ocean and mountains.

During its 1Q25 earnings call, the company noted outperforming rents.

According to CBRE, the SoCal market saw a sequential decline in rents of 4.7% and a year-over-year drop of 12.1%. Meanwhile, Rexford's market rents fell 2.8% sequentially and 9.4% on a year-over-year basis.

Moreover, despite the trade war last month, 80% of its vacancies were under negotiation. I expect that number to bounce back to 90%, which was the number before the trade war went into overdrive.

It also helps that this is a highly fragmented market, with REXR's market share being 2.9%. Through off-market deals and a strong focus on remodeling to improve asset values and rents, it has one of the best track records.

Currently yielding 4.8%, Rexford's dividend has grown by 16% per year over the past five years, one of the best numbers in the entire REIT universe. While dividend growth has come down this year to the low-single-digit range, this is mainly due to short-term market challenges.

Moreover, the company, which has a very healthy balance sheet with a BBB+ rating (one step below the A range) and $1.6 billion in liquidity to, technically speaking, service all maturing debt through 2027, sees 34% in embedded net operating income growth through embedded rent escalators, repositioning, and mark-to-market rent adjustments.

Valuation-wise, we're dealing with a company that trades at 18.1x adjusted funds from operations ("AFFO"). While 2025 is expected to see just 3% AFFO growth, 2026 and 2027 are likely to see 6% and 10% growth, respectively, according to analyst estimates. When adding its dividend, we get an annual total return outlook of 12% without any valuation tailwinds.

Even at just 20x AFFO, we get an annual total return outlook of 15%.

Personally, I expect these numbers to be higher, as the end of the trade war will likely result in stronger trade flows and occupancy rates.

On top of that, I believe in a general rebound of industrial real estate demand in the United States, making Rexford one of my favorite dividend stocks for the next decade (and beyond).

Equity LifeStyle Properties (ELS) - One Of The Most Consistent Dividend Gems On The Market

Equity LifeStyle Properties is a company I have had on my watchlist for many years, as I always considered it one of the best dividend stocks on the market.

The only reason I haven't bought it yet is my focus on more cyclical stocks, which, when I bought them, better fit my risk profile.

That said, ELS has moved to the top of my watchlist for a wide range of reasons. One of these reasons is the relentless stickiness of longer-term rates.

Elevated inflation, geopolitical concerns, and no end in sight to America's deficit problem are reasons why the market isn't giving the U.S. Treasury a break on its funding costs.

This has worked its way into the housing market, as millions have found out the hard way.

Using Freddie Mac data, the average 30-year mortgage rate is hovering close to 7.0%, a number that has been on people's minds since mid-2022, making it one of the most expensive markets to borrow since the early 2000s.

The worst part is that none of this has resulted in lower home prices. Using the latest data, the median asking price is now $431,100 in the United States, 6.4% more compared to a year ago, and the highest number ever.

When adding elevated mortgage rates, we get a horrible situation for homebuyers in an expensive and supply-constrained market.

This is great news for Equity LifeStyle Properties, a company I have always liked because of its unique position in the housing market.

Like Rexford Industrial Realty, it is a REIT, owning manufactured housing, recreational vehicle, and marina assets across 35 states and one Canadian province. These assets include more than 173 thousand sites among 455 properties, allowing the company to return 14% per year since its IPO.

There are so many reasons why I love this business model.

For starters, the company has strong secular growth stemming from general housing affordability issues and the 'Silver Tsunami' of retirees. The company estimates that between now and 2040, the number of people aged 55 and over will rise by 14%, with roughly 10,000 people turning 65 every day for the foreseeable future.

Nearly half of its manufactured housing residents are 70 and older, while more than 70% of its properties are age-qualified for people over the age of 55.

Moreover, the quality of manufactured housing communities has gone up tremendously. We are not talking about the 'trailer parks' you may be thinking of, where you find broken down cars in front of cheap trailers in very dangerous areas, where people who are very unfortunate in life try to make ends meet.

No, MH communities owned by ELS are well-maintained places where most people own MH assets. Instead of rent, they pay lot fees, which pay for amenities like pools, tennis courts, and so much more.

This also makes management easy for ELS, as most residents take care of their own properties. All the company has to do is collect fees and make sure its communities are always in great shape. Generally speaking, this business model allows it to leverage its assets and grow on a consistent basis.

It also helps that MH communities enjoy supply tailwinds, as new construction is pressured by NIMBY (not in my backyard), strict zoning issues, and other planning headwinds.

Another major benefit is cost. While MH communities are not for everyone, they tend to come with up to 66% lower monthly costs compared to the average single-family home.

The worse the housing affordability problem, the bigger the tailwind for ELS.

Here are a few more stats you'll like (I sure do):

  • As of 1Q25, roughly half of its communities had an occupancy rate of more than 98%.
  • All MH contracts are either tied to CPI (inflation), regional market rates, or long-term agreements.
  • All of its RV annual income is tied to market rates.

On a long-term basis, its portfolio has outperformed the cost-of-living increase, with only temporary outliers during 2009, 2012, and 2023.

The same goes for its net operating income. Since 2001, it has outperformed inflation by roughly 2.0% per year, roughly 125 basis points above the residential REIT average. Between 2013 and 2024, core net operating income has grown by 5.1% per year, the best streak in its history.

On top of that, it maintains a bulletproof balance sheet, which comes with a subdued leverage ratio of just 4.4x, a weighted average maturity duration of eight years, a weighted average interest rate of 4.1%, and 98% fixed-rate debt.

Then there's its dividend.

Currently yielding 3.2%, the dividend has a 78% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 9.1%, which beats almost every single REIT on my watchlist.

Over the past ten years, the dividend has grown by nearly 12% per year, three times the REIT industry average.

In other words, while it may not yield 5-6% like some of the REITs you may or may not hold, its dividend growth more than makes up for it, as it often leads to a higher yield on costs after a few years.

While past results are no guarantee of future performance, since its IPO, the company has 'crushed' the S&P 500 and the Dow Jones All-REIT Index by a margin that can be seen from space.

When adding its dividend, I believe it is very hard to beat the mix of safety, income, and growth this stock brings to the table.

That said, since 2019, the ELS stock price hasn't gone anywhere for several reasons. These include its massive rally before and during the pandemic, the subsequent surge in inflation and interest rates, and the resulting negative sentiment towards REITs in general.

Now, it trades at 24.7x AFFO, a number that may seem elevated. However, it is below its ten-year average of 29.3x, as its business model has always warranted a premium among investors.

Going forward, analysts expect 4-7% annual per-share AFFO growth. By including a 26x multiple and its dividend, we get an annual return of at least 11-12% per year going forward.

While elevated rates could keep a lid on ELS for the next few quarters, the risk/reward hasn't been this good in many years, making it a great investment for a wide range of investors.

Depending on my cash situation, I may be a buyer soon, as buying ELS would make a lot of sense in my portfolio, especially because its yield/growth mix could allow me to retire in the future without having to sell ELS.

To me, that's a big deal.

Takeaway

While the market's rally has been impressive, I'm staying grounded, as there isn't much to celebrate, as we are not out of the woods yet.

For now, the rally has been driven more by relief than by renewed fundamentals, and major risks like high valuations and global uncertainty remain.

That's why I'm once again building cash and leaning into quality.

I'm focused on businesses that combine deep value, safety, and long-term income growth potential. This includes Rexford Industrial Realty, which stands to benefit from a trade rebound, and Equity LifeStyle Properties, which I consider to be one of the most reliable dividend stocks around.

If conditions continue to normalize, these names could help drive significant outperformance for years to come, making them two of my favorite dividend investments for the decade ahead (and beyond).

Risks To My Thesis

Risks to my thesis include a renewed escalation in trade tensions, which could pressure Southern California's industrial market and cause general recession risks to rise.

Additionally, higher-for-longer interest rates could continue to challenge REIT valuations and investor sentiment.

For Equity LifeStyle, a weaker consumer or rising operating costs could slow growth.

The good news is that even under severe pressure, both companies should continue to do well.

data.title: JBBB: CLO Market Failing To Recover As Economic Breadth Declines
data.description:
The stock market has had a wild ride during the first half of 2025. Most major indices were down nearly 20% year-to-date early last month, but have since retraced all losses and are now slightly up this year. The sharp rebound is driven by the retraction or delay of most of the "Liberation Day" tariffs and the recent reduction in tariffs on China. It is unclear if tariffs will return in July or if deals will be reached. The stock market has also rebounded due to stronger-than-expected economic data, particularly regarding inflation and employment, which remain stable despite economic concerns.

One market segment that has not rebounded is higher-risk loans. There has been a reasonable improvement in bonds, such as the high-yield bond fund (JNK). Yet, the loan market, represented by the Janus Henderson B-BBB CLO ETF (JBBB), remains down by about 2% this year. That leaves JBBB around a third of the way from fully retracing its ~6% maximal drawdown last month. JBBB offers a high yield of 8% and has lower exposure to changes in interest rates because, unlike JNK or HYG, it is based on floating-rate corporate loans.

Historically, Collateralized Loan Obligations, particularly below-investment-grade CLOs, were only available to institutional and very large investors. Investing in JBBB is nearly as similar to traditional bank lending as retail investors can do. CLOs are securities backed by pools of loans, usually to companies with below investment-grade credit ratings. Often, CLOs lend to companies with single-B or lower credit ratings, which typically have abnormally high default risk.

JBBB takes this further, as it is a pool of CLOs. This means JBBB is not actively managed or focuses on high-value loans, but instead owns a small slice of most of the US non-IG corporate lending market. Although JBBB is a "B-BBB" rated CLO ETF, that does not mean all the companies' loans fall into that credit range; they'll likely be lower. However, the CLOs carry those risk ratings based on their tranche. For example, if defaults rise moderately, A-AAA rated CLOs will still be paid, while B-BBB CLOs will take the hit; however, "equity" CLOs will be unpaid first. JBBB's focus on B to BBB-rated CLOs means it carries similar overall default risk, even though the default risk of the individual underlying loans is usually much higher, mitigated through diversification and tranching.

Its chief risk is a rise in bankruptcy, default, or credit downgrade risk, usually associated with macroeconomic strain. Although the stock market is no longer positioned for significant macroeconomic strain, investors may be wise to consider the macroeconomic data. I believe the hard and soft economic data are more critical for JBBB than other stocks and bond funds because it is more exposed to the riskiest segment of the corporate lending market.

Corporate Loans Offer Historically Low Yields

When considering CLOs, we must focus on the highest risk segments of the lending market. JBBB will decline in value if there is a rise, or an expected increase, in defaults or downgrades for most companies with very low credit ratings. Because B-BBB CLOs are diversified and tranched, JBBB should only see negative pressure if systemic risk facing high-risk companies increases.

This point is nuanced and essential. For example, if a B-BBB-rated CLO owns debt to one overleveraged oil company, and a lower oil price drives it into default, that CLO may not see a loss, as a lower tranche will absorb that (so long as the majority of the CLO is not in similar firms). Further, a B-BBB-rated CLO may decline even if most of the economy is OK, as high-risk sectors often face hardship even if offset by growing sectors. This has been the case in recent years, as technology companies drive most economic growth, while many manufacturing and labor-heavy firms have faced hardship.

Today, a few sectors dominate the single B and lower credit markets. As has been the case for years, that includes telecommunications, which suffers from naturally high leverage and increasing competition. The media and entertainment sector has increasingly dominated the B-segment, mainly due to the long-lasting headwinds triggered in 2020; similar to telecommunications, the media sector is struggling with cord-cutting. Healthcare services have also fallen into this segment as higher wages have pushed operating costs higher, while healthcare providers are unwilling to pay more.

Again, JBBB's chief risk includes a general recession, but is not limited to that. For example, over the past decade, I argue that companies with low labor requirements (more highly skilled labor) have driven most economic growth. In contrast, those with high labor overhead (unskilled labor) have stagnated and are increasingly struggling with lower demand for low-paying work. Thus, the economy is bifurcating, driving credit risk for stagnant segments, despite decent overall growth.

This shift can be seen in the gap in hourly real output, the fundamental measure of economic development, between manufacturing and overall business:

Non-farm business includes manufacturing, so the fact that overall non-farm business output has risen while manufacturing has stalled for the past twenty years indicates significant growth in non-manufacturing, like technology and services. Notably, manufacturing output rose in Q1, likely due to temporarily higher demand from tariff frontrunning (see imports surge in Q1), while overall output fell slightly. Although that is just one quarter of declines, it is an ominous economic signal.

There is also the issue of low consumer sentiment. Although GDP growth has been decent while unemployment has been low, consumer sentiment has been abysmally low, starting in 2020. One overlooked factor driving this may be the extremely low labor share of the GDP, which is the portion of GDP that goes to total employee compensation. Until the 1990s, the labor share was generally around two-thirds of the GDP, with the remaining third going to capital (profits to investors). That figure has since declined to about 55%, likely driven by the rise in automation and outsourcing of domestic labor to lower-cost countries. Notably, this figure has rebounded around the late 2010s, which saw strong sentiment. However, in the wake of 2020 and the inflation since, we've seen a sharp decline in labor share and consumer sentiment, which are often correlated. See below:

In my view, in assessing JBBB's risk, we must primarily consider these big-picture economic trends. A recession and decline in corporate profits an obvious risk factors to JBBB, but that is generally accounted for in its 8% yield. However, its long-term relative performance is likely driven by the relative performance of underperforming economic segments.

Based on the above long-term data, I think it's reasonable to say that real economic trends drive today's low consumer sentiment. It is not a "vibecession" (misplaced pessimism), as some economists argue, but a real, long-lasting trend wherein weaker economic segments (and people who work in them) have been seeing increasing pain. In contrast, strong segments (like technology) have performed so well that the overall economy appears fine, even if top performers increasingly skew it.

In my view, the underperforming segment of the economy is likely facing greater risk increases today than in recent years. For example, we're seeing a potentially steady rise in delinquencies on commercial and industrial loans. That figure is 1.3% today, which is not high, but could soon become high if it continues to trend higher. Credit spreads on CCC and below corporate loans (likely akin to many of those JBBB owns) are only slightly elevated, having retraced after the recent correction. See below:

Overall yields on CCC and below debt may seem fine, averaging around 13% today, which is higher than typical. However, that rise is largely due to the rise in Treasury rates, as the spread between CCC debt yields and Treasuries is ~8.8%, which is lower compared to recent years despite rising delinquencies. In my view, this implies that JBBB's 8% yield, though seemingly high, may not fully compensate for rising risks on these loans.

Of course, because of the similarity in credit risk profiles, JBBB has a strong inverse correlation to CCC or below spreads:

If this spread metric continues to decline, we may expect JBBB's price to rise back to ~$ $49. However, I would be surprised to see CCC spreads fall back to 7%, given there are no clear signs of economic strength rebounding in underperforming segments. To me, this indicates excessive investor interest in high-yield loans, with less regard to underlying risk.

Some analysts may point out that JBBB has outperformed bond funds such as JNK that carry similar yields. That is mainly because JBBB is a floating rate, while JNK is not. As the Fed hikes rates, interest on JBBB's underlying debt increases, improving its yield and raising default risks as companies pay more in interest.

We can also compare JBBB to the more aggressive CLO fund, XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (XFLT), which owns a higher portion of equity-tranche CLOs. XFLT's yield is very high, usually 15-20%, due to its higher risk. Notably, XFLT has seen a larger decline this year and has had an even worse rebound than JBBB, almost failing to recover. See below:

I believe XFLT's underperformance may be a warning sign for JBBB. If defaults rise, many of XFLT's equity CLOs will be the first not to be paid. If they continue to increase, more of JBBB's B-BBB grade CLOs will not be paid.

Regarding JBBB's outperformance to JNK, that has less to do with differences in risk or management, but changes in rates. Their total return ratio shows that JBBB only outperformed JNK in 2023, as interest rates had risen significantly. See below:

Their performance has been about identical since 2024. Given that overall inflation and economic growth are unclear, the market is not bracing for cuts or hikes.

The Bottom Line

Overall, I am moderately bearish on JBBB due to the high risk of economic strain in weaker segments of the economy. I think a recession is possible, but it will potentially be avoided by continued economic growth in top-performing segments such as technology. However, considering trade uncertainty may rise in July and tariffs are still in effect, while consumer sentiment and wage power are low, and output slipped in Q1, I think it's undeniable that a decent portion of the US economy is under strain today.

For JBBB, it generally does not matter if technology giants perform well, as it has low exposure to those firms. However, JBBB will face pressure if corporations already struggling with high debt and margin compression face greater strains, which, I believe, is likely based on the data. Further, JBBB seems likelier to see a lower yield from potential rate cuts than outperformance due to a shift to rate hikes. Inflation is likely not high enough to warrant rate hikes, though employment is strong sufficient that significant cuts seem unlikely.

JBBB's 8% yield may seem attractive, but it does not offer a high spread above Treasuries given its elevated risk exposure in the current economic climate. There are many factors I can point to, but the greatest is likely if Trump holds to the 90-day pause, meaning we may be back to where we were a month ago by July. Though Trump may entirely back down from tariffs, the fact that stocks are almost back to all-time-highs indicates they're not pricing in the risk that he will not. The fact that speculative-grade corporate debt spreads are so low suggests a lack of pricing for this risk factor.

Finally, I see the decline in XFLT as a potential red flag. JBBB's credit risk is very non-linear, in that losses are negligible until defaults reach a threshold. The further XFLT's equity CLOs fall, the closer JBBB's CLOs will likely reach that threshold. The "cliff effect" in JBBB may soon arise, particularly if the recent rebound in risk assets does not hold steady over the coming weeks.

data.title: Logan Ridge Finance: Poor Credit Quality Makes This 8% Yielding BDC One To Avoid
data.description:
Introduction

If you've been investing for some time now, then you've probably made a few mistakes along the way. It happens to the best of us. And companies in the stock market are no exception.

To quote a favorite movie of mine:

Just because someone stumbles and loses their way, doesn't mean they're lost forever.

When analyzing dividend companies, I try to look at them from an unbiased point of view. No matter how great a company is, they will face challenges. But it's how they navigate those challenges over the long term that tells me if that company is worthy of investment.

In the case of Logan Ridge Finance Corporation (NASDAQ:LRFC), the business development company, or BDC, has faced some tough challenges over the past few years, as you can see from their share price performance.

And despite a potential merger with another BDC, which I'll touch on in the article that could bring benefit, LRFC is one I would avoid for now.

In this article, I discuss their latest quarter, overall credit quality, and why I would avoid Logan Ridge Finance Corp despite their attractive high yield.

Previous Hold Thesis

I covered LRFC this past January in an article titled: Disappointing Performance Warrants Huge Discount To NAV. During their then Q3 earnings, the BDC experienced a decline in both its top & bottom lines.

Net investment income of $0.43 safely covered their quarterly distribution of $0.36 giving them solid dividend coverage of 119%.

But total investment income and net asset value declined, which could have been attributed to lower base rates, but was likely mainly due to their questionable credit quality.

Since then, Logan Ridge Finance Corp has continued to be a disappointing investment, down an additional 24% in comparison to the S&P 500 (SP500), which is down closer to 3% at the time of writing.

Questionable Credit Quality

Logan Ridge Finance Corp reported their first quarter earnings earlier this month with net investment income of $0.35, or $0.9 million and total investment income amounting to $4.6 million.

Both declined from the previous quarter's $0.56, or $1.5 million and $5.4 million respectively, with lower base rates and non-recurring income being contributing factors.

However, their overall credit quality remains in question as the BDC was forced to write-down one of their legacy investments, Sequoia, for $4.4 million. As a result, this also negatively impacted their net asset value, which continued on a sequential decline.

NAV stood at $29.66, a significant decline of 7.4% from the previous quarter. Year-over-year, this fell further, by double-digits from $33.71, again bringing into question LRFC's credit quality.

Logan Ridge Finance's management changed in 2021 and has been trying to right the ship ever since. The current macro environment has made this more difficult, with interest rates hiked their fastest in history from 2022 to 2024.

And while higher base rates typically serve as tailwinds for BDCs, this is sort of a double-edged sword.

Higher interest rates bring in more income for BDCs as a result of their predominantly floating-rate portfolios. On the flip side, this could negatively impact their borrowers, especially those with lower credit quality.

This is evident by Logan Ridge Finance's non-accruals, which stood at 8.7% and 2.2% on a cost & fair value basis. Year-over-year, these declined on a fair value basis from 5.3%, but rose on a cost basis from 8.3%.

In total, 4 companies were currently on non-accrual status, including Sequoia, which has been on the non-accrual list for the last 4 years.

It's not out of the norm for BDCs to see companies added to non-accrual status, especially during tough economic times. And some borrowers face seasonality challenges.

But 4 years is a long time for any company to be non-performing without a BDC's management team successfully exiting the investment or restructuring it back to accrual status.

For comparison, peer Great Elm Capital Corp. (GECC), in their latest quarter, has 0 investments on non-accrual status after successfully restructuring one borrower this past February. The year prior, GECC's non-accruals accounted for roughly 2% of the portfolio at fair value.

Investment Activity

One good thing Logan Ridge Finance Corp has done in the past year is take a more defensive position. First-lien loans accounted for 67.6% of the total portfolio, up from 64.7% the previous quarter.

And this increased from 65.2% in the previous year's quarter. However, this doesn't equate to a better performance going forward, as seen by continued borrower challenges. During the quarter, LRFC invested $15.1 million in new & existing investments, bringing their total portfolio value to $169.6 million across 59 companies.

Their portfolio could also be considered attractive from an investment standpoint as Healthcare (XLV) & Technology (XLK) account for most of their portfolio at 20.3% and 15.4% respectively.

Pending Merger

In January, Logan Ridge Finance Corp entered into an agreement with another BDC, Portman Ridge Finance Corp. (PTMN) for a potential merger. This is expected to provide size & scale advantages as well as liquidity benefits.

The merger is also expected to positively impact top and bottom lines, as well as NAV. But the merger is still subject to approval and projected to be approved sometime in the second quarter of this year. And while it will likely provide benefits going forward, the current macro environment will likely continue to present challenges.

Dividend Coverage

As a result of their performance during their latest quarter that saw LRFC write down an investment, net investment income failed to cover the quarterly dividend of $0.36.

This was in comparison to Portman Ridge Finance's 100% dividend coverage. Great Elm Capital had the highest coverage of the peer group at 108%.

While I do think Logan Ridge Finance Corp's balance sheet gives them flexibility with no debt maturing until October 2026, this is something investors should keep a close eye on in the coming quarters.

Moreover, while a potential dividend cut could already be priced in here, LRFC's high probability of a dividend cut over the next 4 to 6 months is still a risk that could have them see further downside.

Especially if the merger fails to get approved, but I think there's a high probability it will. Their leverage was higher than the peer average of 1.19x at 1.2x.

But they did have liquidity comprised of $5.1 million in cash and $31.5 million available in revolver capacity, also giving them flexibility to sustain the current dividend for the near-term.

Discount To NAV

At the current price, Logan Ridge's discount to NAV could be considered attractive. Additionally, their dividend yield over 8% is also enticing if you're an income investor.

At their current discount of nearly 40%, this is higher than their 3-year average of 36.82%, an indication of the BDC's current troubles.

As a result, I would like to see stable financials and NAV as well as portfolio growth before considering upgrading them to a buy.

Risks & Bottom Line

Aside from a potential dividend cut, Logan Ridge Finance Corp could see non-accruals rise further in the near future, increasing chances of a dividend cut.

The latest quarter showed negative GDP growth for the first time in 3 years. And this could be a sign that the economy is finally cracking, despite appearing resilient.

The upcoming merger with Portman Ridge Finance Corp is a good move for both BDCs as they have been negatively impacted by higher for longer interest rates.

With both being smaller, they lack size & liquidity advantages of larger peers, resulting in declining financials, followed by a lower share price.

And while Logan Ridge Finance Corp could benefit long-term, their poor performance and questionable credit quality makes this BDC one I would avoid for now.

data.title: REGL Is A Safer Way To Play The Mid-Cap Space Than MDY
data.description:
For many investors, mid-cap stocks are a forgotten segment of the market. Major news outlets constantly follow large-cap stocks through their reporting of the Mag 7 or the broader S&P 500. Small-cap stocks are often touted for their potential for large gains that come with elevated risk. Meanwhile, most mid-cap companies and mid-cap stock funds fly under the radar. This happens despite their track record of strong returns over long periods of time.

In March, American Century issued a review of mid-cap stocks, calling them the “unappreciated middle child” and noting their historical advantages over both large and small-caps. Since the inception of the Russell Midcap Index in 1978, mid-cap stocks have outperformed large-caps by 40% and crushed small-cap returns by over 100%. While large companies have shown significant advantages in returns over the last few years, mid-caps are once again on top for the first four and a half months of 2025 when the Russell indices are used.

The ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrat ETF (BATS:REGL) is a unique way to participate in the mid-cap segment of the market. Rather than following the entire mid-cap index, this fund follows a basket of only 53 stocks that have shown a history of growing dividends. Nine of its 53 stocks have increased their dividend for 42 years or more.

In full disclosure, I have owned this fund for several years, and I spent the last few days making sure that this investment still belonged in my account. I consider myself a conservative investor, but I also want my account to benefit from capital appreciation. After conducting my research, I am pleased to see that this fund is still a buy based on the analysis presented here.

Fund Description and Past Performance

Launched in February of 2015, the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrat ETF is a passively managed fund that is designed to follow the performance of the S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats Index. This index tracks the performance of companies that belong to the S&P MidCap 400 that have raised their dividend payouts at least once a year for 15 years or more. Companies included in the S&P MidCap 400 generally have a median market cap around $7 billion.

REGL contains all 53 stocks from the index it follows, and the fund is designed to have an equal-weighted portfolio. The fund is rebalanced quarterly and updated with new stocks that meet the dividend requirement on an annual basis. ProShares states that the fund must hold a minimum of 40 stocks. If 40 such stocks do not exist, the managers may select the next closest qualifiers. Because the fund does not have a heavy concentration in a small portion of its portfolio, it is not dependent on just a few holdings to drive the bulk of its returns.

REGL has $1.73 billion in assets under management. Its top 12 holdings as of May 9 are listed below. The difference in weight for the top stock on the list and the smallest holding is only 0.54%. The smallest share of the portfolio currently belongs to Lancaster Colony Corporation (LANC), a food service company that lost over 15% of its value on April 30 after a disappointing earnings report.

REGL has had a remarkable track record in its first 10 years of existence. The fund has ranked in the top 1% of the funds in its category in two of the nine years that it has been tracked by Morningstar – 2016 and 2018. It was ranked in the top 2% in 2022. However, the fund seems to have somewhat of a “feast or famine” past, as the fund was also in the bottom 10% for its category in 2019 and 2023. In 2024, Morningstar changed the comparison index for the fund from mid-cap blend to small-cap value. Like a prize-fighting boxer that competes in a new weight class, the ProShares S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrat ETF is once again in the top percentile for performance when compared to its new category, year-to-date.

In the fund profile, ProShares claims that REGL is a more conservative way to play the mid-cap sector. However, these wide swings in rankings from Morningstar deserve a more thorough explanation. REGL's sector weightings are listed below, copied from the fund website. Financials make up the largest share of the fund with a current weight of 30.79%. Industrials and Utilities follow with 19.2% and 18.2% respectively. The fund currently allocates 0% of its portfolio to stocks in the communication services and technology sectors. Energy just barely makes the cut with 1.92%, which can be solely attributed to the fund’s ownership of DT Midstream (DTM), a stock that has only joined the fund in the last year.

This complete lack of exposure to technology and other high-growth stocks helps to explain why the fund can whip around in the Morningstar rankings so dramatically. While large-cap, Mag 7 stocks would never be included in a mid-cap stock fund, there are other smaller tech players in that segment of the market. For example, Okta (OKTA), Guidewire Software (GWRE), and DocuSign (DOCU) are all among the top 10 holdings in the S&P MidCap 400 Index, and these companies are part of the Technology sector which makes up almost 13% of that index. OKTA has risen over 26% in the last 12 months. GWRE has gained 84%, while DOCU is up 47%. REGL has not been able to benefit from any of those gains. On the flip side, when the market favors other sectors, REGL shareholders are rewarded.

The fund has an expense ratio of 0.40%, which is relatively high for a passively managed fund, and has a yield of 2.55%. The dividend growth rate for the last 12 months is 20.4%. Over the life of the fund, the compound average growth rate for the dividend is 12.2%.

The Case For REGL

There are three main reasons why I will continue to believe that REGL is an ETF worth buying. The first is the quality and current value of mid-cap stocks. In a market update from February of this year, ProShares reported that the stocks in the S&P Mid-Cap 400 were trading at about 60% of the valuation of large-cap stocks. The fund manager also reported that 75% of mid-cap company revenues were derived from domestic sources, making the possible impact from tariffs less problematic.

Below is a graph that shows the performance of mid-cap stocks since 1991. Not only have mid-caps outperformed over the full-time period, but they have also topped the returns for the S&P 500 in over 75% of separate rolling 10-year periods.

My second key supporting argument for REGL comes from a comparison between its returns at the broader mid-cap index which can be represented by the SPDR® S&P MIDCAP 400 ETF Trust (MDY). Below is a chart showing the returns of REGL and MDY over five different time periods. Returns were measured after the close of the market on May 12.

REGL

REGL wins for the YTD, one-year, and ten-year periods. MDY wins for the three and five-year spans. It is important to note that REGL does this while also generating a dividend yield that is almost twice as large as the yield for MDY. Over the last 10 years, MDY also has a much smaller compound average dividend growth rate at only 8.8%.

Last, but definitely not least, REGL has been able to generate its returns in a relatively conservative way. Since it was created in 2015, the returns of the S&P MidCap 400 Dividend Aristocrats Index have beaten the returns of the larger S&P MidCap 400 with lower volatility. While REGL has been able to capture only 87% of the upside of the broader index, it has been shielded from greater losses, suffering only 77% of the downside. I see no reason that this trend will not continue when I look at the portfolio's key financial metrics as shown in the chart below. Each one seems to favor REGL over MDY.

Downside Risks to Consider

REGL is particularly sensitive to higher interest rates, and there are many signs that rates may stay higher for longer this year. Financials make up almost one-third of this fund, and higher interest rates may compress their profits margins as borrowers avoid higher loan costs. Utility stocks are the third-largest sector in the fund, and those stocks also tend to do poorly in higher rate environments. Many utility stock investors are drawn to the dividends that those stocks pay, but higher interest rates make those dividends less attractive on a relative basis when compared to other investment options.

The management fee for REGL is unusually high for an ETF that is passively managed. Higher fees over time will impact the long-term growth prospects for the fund. As a point of comparison with the other funds mentioned in this article, MDY has a management fee of 0.24%. Another alternative in this space, the Vanguard Mid-Cap Index Fund (VO) charges investors only 0.04%. Previous returns seem to support the higher fee, but, of course, past performance is no guarantee of future success.

Seeking Alpha’s Quant rating for the fund is a hold, but it is below the midpoint on its scale, making it closer to a sell than a buy. One of the biggest detractors is Risk, which is affected by that “feast or famine” trend that I noted earlier. I expect that score, along with the score for Momentum, to improve if the fund continues its strong relative performance from this year.

Conclusion

Investors who are looking for flashier sectors of the market such as large cap growth or technology should consider alternatives to this fund. REGL is a more conservative way to participate in the US equities market with companies that have an extended history of returning an increasing amount of their profits to their shareholders. The current valuation of this fund along with its history of outperformance over the last decade gives me confidence that this portfolio of stocks is still a worthy investment choice. I rate the stock as a buy for investors with a long-term horizon for total returns.
